questions.question: Compositional balance is about…
options: Distributing visual weight across the frame so it feels stable
Keeping the camera level on a tripod
Setting equal aperture and shutter values
Balancing the white balance and tint
questions.reviewHint: Balance is the distribution of visual weight so the image feels stable rather than lopsided.

questions.question: 'Symmetrical' (formal) balance is achieved when…
options: Elements mirror each other across an axis
One large element sits alone on one side
The subject is always off-centre
The horizon is tilted
questions.reviewHint: Symmetrical balance mirrors elements across a central axis for a formal, stable feel.

questions.question: 'Asymmetrical' balance creates a more…
options: Dynamic feel using different elements of equal visual weight
Static and formal feel
Blurred result
Underexposed result
questions.reviewHint: Asymmetry balances unlike elements of similar visual weight for a dynamic look.

questions.question: What is 'visual mass' (visual weight)?
options: How much an element draws the eye, based on size, tone and colour
The physical weight of the camera
The file size of the image
The focal length in millimetres
questions.reviewHint: Visual mass is how strongly an element attracts attention - larger, darker or brighter elements weigh more.

questions.question: A small bright object can balance a large dark one because…
options: Brightness gives it visual weight comparable to the larger element
Small objects always dominate
Dark objects have no weight
Size is the only factor in balance
questions.reviewHint: Bright or saturated elements carry visual weight, so a small bright object can offset a larger one.

questions.question: The golden ratio in composition is associated with…
options: A spiral and grid placement that feels naturally balanced
A fixed shutter speed
A specific ISO value
The crop factor of a sensor
questions.reviewHint: The golden ratio (and golden spiral / phi grid) offers placement that feels naturally harmonious.

questions.question: The 'phi grid' is similar to the rule-of-thirds grid but…
options: Its lines are positioned closer to the centre
It uses only one line
It is always diagonal
It only works in portrait orientation
questions.reviewHint: The phi grid resembles thirds but with the dividing lines nearer the centre.

questions.question: When is breaking symmetry a good idea?
options: When a deliberate asymmetry adds interest or focus
Never - symmetry must always be kept
Only in black and white
Only at high ISO
questions.reviewHint: Breaking symmetry intentionally can add tension, interest or emphasis.

questions.question: Colour can affect balance because…
options: Saturated or warm colours carry more visual weight than muted ones
Colour has no effect on balance
Only size matters
Only tone matters
questions.reviewHint: Colour contributes to visual weight - vivid colours pull the eye and must be balanced.

questions.question: Balancing elements of different sizes usually means…
options: Arranging them so their combined visual weight feels even
Making every element the same size
Removing all but one element
Placing everything in one corner
questions.reviewHint: Harmony comes from arranging unequal elements so their visual weights offset across the frame.
